coredata.dc:title: All-in-one photocatalysis device for one-step high concentration H2O2 photoproduction
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Zhenzhen
Zhao, Yajie
Zhou, Yunjie
Wang, Xiao
Huang, Hui
Liu, Yang
Shao, Mingwang
Kang, Zhenhui
coredata.dc:description: Achieving multi-functions integrating reaction, separation and concentration into one-step catalysis process is a great challenge. Photocatalytic H2O2 generation is the cleanest and cheapest way for its production. Herein, an all-in-one photocatalytic device for H2O2 generation was designed by catalysis layer with ZIF-8/C3N4 composite and carbon dots-based evaporation layer (CDE). With this device, H2O2 solution with high concentration is obtained, and no need for extra separation and concentration processes. Under 2 sun illumination (3 h), H2O2 evolution reaches 17.13 μmol and H2O2 solution concentration gets to 10.15 mmol L−1. Mole fraction of H2O2 solution obtained by all-in-one device is 1.86 times more concentrated than that of not concentrated H2O2 solution. Besides, multiples devices can work concurrently to realize large-scale production and gain large amounts of H2O2 solution with high concentration. Moreover, all-in-one devices can be reused at least ten cycles. The relation between the structure and performance of the photocatalysis device is further studied by COMSOL Multiphysics software and mathematical analysis. All-in-one photocatalytic device promotes H2O2 photoproduction closer to the practical application due to realizing continuous production of amounts of aqueous solution of H2O2 with high concentration in one-step.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustrative schematic of all-in-one photocatalysis device (a) and its fabrication process (b).

Morphology and structure of ZCN catalyst. (a) TEM, (b) HAADF-STEM image and the elemental mapping images for (c) C, (d) N, and (e) Zn elements of ZCN. (f) XRD pattern of C3N4 (black curve), ZIF-8 (blue curve), and ZCN (red curve). High-resolution XPS spectra of (g) Zn 2p, (h) C 1s and (i) N 1s in ZCN.

Characterization of all-in-one catalysis device. (a) Digital photo of all-in-one catalysis device. (b) TEM image (inset is the HRTEM image and size distribution) of CDs. (c) SEM image of ZCN (insert is the digital photo of ZCN). (d) Changes in the amount of H2O2 generated under AM1.5G simulated sunlight (1 sun) irradiation with catalyst dispersed in water directly (blue line) or fixed on the cellulose paper (red line). (e) The mass changes over time with CDs-based evaporator (red line) or bulk water (blue line) under AM1.5G simulated sunlight irradiation.

H2O2 photoproduction with a single all-in-one catalytic device. (a) Dependence of H2O2 solution concentration (blue curve) and H2O2 evolution (red curve) versus reaction time under full spectrum irradiation (1 sun) with a single all-in-one device of the CDE height of 3 cm (photocatalysis area of 7.5360 cm2 and evaporation area of 4.7728 cm2). (b) Plot showing the temperature of the tip, bottom of CDE and surface of catalyst layer under 1 sun relative to the irradiation time. (c) Digital photo of all-in-one photocatalytic device (left), thermal images of all-in-one photocatalytic device before illumination (middle) and after illumination for 40 min (right) under 1 sun.

Photocatalysis performance analysis with a single all-in-one device. (a) H2O2 solution concentration (blue curve) and H2O2 evolution (red curve) versus the height of evaporators of 0, 1 cm, 3 cm and 5 cm under full spectrum irradiation (1 sun) for 3 h. (b) Plot showing the temperature of CDE tip at different CDE heights under 1 sun relative to irradiation time. (c) Multiples of H2O2 concentration (mole fraction) with evaporators (height of 3 cm) comparing with no evaporators under full spectrum irradiation with different solar flux for 3 h. (d) Cycling stability (CDE height of 3 cm) for the generation of H2O2 under full spectrum irradiation (1 sun). Each cycle keeps 3 h.

Photocatalysis performance with four all-in-one devices (CDE height of 3 cm). (a) H2O2 solution concentration (blue curve) and H2O2 evolution (red curve) versus the number of devices under full spectrum irradiation (1 sun) for 3 h. (b) Dependence of H2O2 solution concentration (blue curve) and H2O2 evolution (red curve) versus reaction time under 1 sun with quadruple all-in-one structures. (c) Digital photo (left) and thermal images of the quadruple all-in-one structure before illumination (middle) and after illumination (right) for 40 min under 1 sun.

Photocatalysis performance of 2D all-in-one structures. (a) H2O2 solution concentration (blue curve) and H2O2 evolution (red curve) versus increasing proportions of the catalysis area. (b) H2O2 solution concentration (blue curve) and H2O2 evolution (red curve) with catalysis layer (work area of 7.0650 cm2) loaded by catalyst, ZCN and photothermal materials, CDs mixed directly. (c) Plot showing the temperature change of the catalyst surface containing the different content of CDs (0%, 16.7% and 37.5%) under 1 sun relative to the irradiation time. (d) Thermal images of the corresponding samples containing the different content of CDs before illumination and after illumination for 40 min under 1 sun.

Mechanism of 3D all-in-one device. (a) Theoretical simulation of the H2O2 concentration distribution with the radius of the evaporation cone of 0.50 cm under 1 sun illumination after 3 h. (b) Theoretical simulation of the temperature distribution with the radius of the evaporation cone of 0.50 cm under 1 sun illumination after 3 h. (c) H2O2 concentration changes in remaining solution using varied 3D all-in-one devices with different radius of the evaporation cone under 1 sun illumination. (d) H2O2 concentration and evolution of both experimental and simulative value with all-in-one devices of different radii of the evaporation cone under 1 sun illumination after 3 h. (e) Dependence of H2O2 solution concentration versus reaction time according to experimental value (red curve), simulative value (black curve) and theoretical value (blue curve), respectively.

head.ce:title: All-in-one photocatalysis device for one-step high concentration H2O2 photoproduction

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: All-in-one photocatalysis device is used for one-step high concentration H2O2 photoproduction.

Achieving multi-functions integrating reaction, separation and concentration into one-step catalysis process is a great challenge. Photocatalytic H2O2 generation is the cleanest and cheapest way for its production. Herein, an all-in-one photocatalytic device for H2O2 generation was designed by catalysis layer with ZIF-8/C3N4 composite and carbon dots-based evaporation layer (CDE). With this device, H2O2 solution with high concentration is obtained, and no need for extra separation and concentration processes. Under 2 sun illumination (3 h), H2O2 evolution reaches 17.13 μmol and H2O2 solution concentration gets to 10.15 mmol L−1. Mole fraction of H2O2 solution obtained by all-in-one device is 1.86 times more concentrated than that of not concentrated H2O2 solution. Besides, multiples devices can work concurrently to realize large-scale production and gain large amounts of H2O2 solution with high concentration. Moreover, all-in-one devices can be reused at least ten cycles. The relation between the structure and performance of the photocatalysis device is further studied by COMSOL Multiphysics software and mathematical analysis. All-in-one photocatalytic device promotes H2O2 photoproduction closer to the practical application due to realizing continuous production of amounts of aqueous solution of H2O2 with high concentration in one-step.

ce:section.ce:para: Catalysis is a typical interdisciplinary comprehensive cross-science, and modern catalysis always demands the development of new concepts, materials, catalysis processes as well as equipment. The development in the field of modern catalysis always faces numerous challenges, such as working out prominent issues in energy, environment [1–7] and chemical production, and addressing the complexity of catalysis research. Notably, modern catalysis is expected to achieve multi-functions simultaneously for integrating reaction, separation and concentration into one-step process [8,9].

Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), as a significant clean liquid fuel, is produced through several main approaches in industry, including anthraquinone oxidation process, electrochemical synthesis, as well as oxidation of alcohol [10,11], etc. However, those methods are always along with high energy consumption, complicated process and serious pollution, which inspires researchers to find an eco-friendly way to produce H2O2. Since photocatalysis is the cleanest and cheapest way to produce H2O2, lots of catalysts have been studied, like ZIF-8/C3N4 composite [12], Au/BiVO4 [13], rGO/TiO2 [14], carbon nitride-aromatic diimide-graphene nanohybrids [15], 3DOM g-C3N4-PW11 [16] and C3N4-based composite [17–20], etc. Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), and covalent organic frameworks (COFs), are crystalline hybrid materials with high porosity, tunable structure and strong gas adsorption capacity, and have been vastly used as photocatalysts and photothermal materials [21–24]. ZIF-8, as a typical MOF material, abundant pore structure is beneficial to the mass transfer, diffusion of H2O2, and continuous generation of H2O2 as well. C3N4 is a promising organic semiconductor photocatalyst to drive water splitting under visible light owing to its proper band structure, superior physicochemical stability, and high abundance [25–27]. Among current reported photocatalysts for H2O2 production, ZIF-8/C3N4 composite (ZCN) exhibits quite high H2O2 conversion rate with water as reactant without any scavenger. Here, ZIF-8/C3N4 is an ideal catalyst to assemble all-in-one photocatalytic devices to achieve one-step production of high concentration H2O2 without further separation and concentration steps.

Solar steam generation has gained great interest as an efficient approach to purify seawater and other applications, such as water purification [28–31], energy generation [32–35], and water transport [36], etc. However, few researches are conducted to study a system combining photocatalysis process and water evaporation simultaneously. On one hand, mixing photocatalyst with photothermic materials directly is detrimental to the photocatalysis process because the relatively high temperature accelerates the recombination of electrons and holes [37–39]. And on the other hand, adding catalysts to photothermic materials also leads to degradation of evaporation performance. [40,41] Hence, designing a delicate device remaining respective awesome properties and improving the utilization of solar energy is a great challenge. Carbon dots (CDs) [42] are one of carbon materials with great photothermal properties, and CDs-based evaporators show great water evaporation ability with robust and excellent deformation tolerance properties, which is quite suitable to construct the water evaporation part of all-in-one photocatalysis device [43].

Herein, an assembled photocatalytic device integrating reaction, separation and concentration, was designed and fabricated by catalysis layer with ZCN as the photocatalyst and CDs-based evaporation layer (CDE) (Fig. 1 a, b). For conventional solid–liquid photocatalysis, product separation from reaction system and catalyst recycle by centrifugation or filtration is a must, and loss of catalyst is inevitable, while just taking away the device to realize separation process is pretty convenient. And with all-in-one device, H2O2 evolution reaches 17.13 μmol and H2O2 solution concentration gets to 10.15 mmol L−1 under 2 sun illumination for 3 h. Mole fraction of H2O2 solution obtained through all-in-one device working is 1.86 times more concentrated than that of not concentrated H2O2 solution. Besides, multiples devices can work concurrently to realize large-scale production of H2O2 solution with high concentration. All-in-one photocatalytic device promotes H2O2 photoproduction closer to the practical application due to realizing continuous production of a large amount of H2O2 solution with high concentration in one-step process. And it can be further applied for virus inactivation and pollutant degradation due to the presence of reactive oxygen species (such as H2O2, ·OH, ·O2 −, etc.) during the photocatalysis process [44,45]. The design of this photocatalytic device gives an example to integrate reaction, separation and concentration in one-step in the field of photocatalysis systems requiring liquid product separation, and expands the application of water evaporation.

Graphite rods (99.99%) was purchased from Alfa Aesar Co., Ltd. Hydrazine hydrate was obtained from Chinasun Specialty Products Co., Ltd. Carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) was bought from Shanghai Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co., Ltd. Zinc acetate dihydrate (Zn (CH3COO)2·2H2O; 99%), 2-methylimidazole (C4H6N2, H-MeIM; 99%), hexadecyl trimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB, 99%) and urea (CO(NH2)2; 99%) are all purchased from Adamas Reagent Co., Ltd. All the reagents were tested directly without further treatment.

CDs were prepared by an electrochemically etching method. Two high-purity graphite rods were cleaned through ultrasonic treatment in ultrapure water three times, and each ultrasonic treatment kept 10 min. Then the two rods, as anode and cathode, were inserted in 1 L ultrapure water in parallel with 7.5 cm intervals, and a constant voltage of 30 V was applied under violent agitation. This process was completed until the anode graphite rod was fully etched. Eventually, the CDs powder was obtained after filter the dark dispersion liquid, centrifugation at 12000 rpm for 30 min and freeze-drying.

In a typical photocatalyst synthesis, ZCN catalysts (the mixture of ZIF-8 and C3N4) was synthesized through a simple method developed by our group previously [12]. For the synthesis of ZIF-8, 0.30 g zinc acetate dihydrate was first dissolved in 5 mL deionized (DI) water. Then 2.23 g 2-methylimidazole and 2 mg cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) were dissolved in another 5 mL DI water. After that zinc acetate dihydrate solution and 2-methylimidazole solution were mixed and stirred 10 min, then stood 2 h at room temperature. After synthesis, the ZIF-8 was collected by centrifuging at 12000 rpm for 20 min, washing with water 3 times, and then drying at 65 °C in a vacuum. Carbon nitride (C3N4) was synthesized by one-step calcination in a muffle furnace. Specifically, 30 g urea was put into a ceramic crucible, and then calcined to 550 °C for 3 h with a heating rate of 10 °C min−1 in a muffle furnace. C3N4 was obtained after cooling down to room temperature. ZCN was prepared through mix ZIF-8 and C3N4 fully (mass ratio, 1.5: 1), after that the mixture was put into a tubular furnace and kept at 400 °C for 120 min under N2 atmosphere with the heating rate of 5 °C min−1. The products were collected after cooling to room temperature.

All-in-one photocatalytic structure was fabricated with bilayer structure. The bottom layer was a truncated cone fabricated with a piece of annulus-like cellulose paper by connecting the ring width, and the top layer was a cone made of sector-like cellulose paper by connecting the radius. The bottom layer was catalysis layer, the top layer was the light-absorbing layer, and the joint was separated by a short polyethylene tube to avoid mass and heat transfer between two layers. 40 mg CDs powder and 5 mg CMC were mixed in ultrapure water. The CDs-based evaporator (CDE) was prepared by blade casting the CDs slurry on cellulose paper uniformly, and followed by thermal treatment with hydrazine hydrate vapor under 90 °C for 3 h. Catalysis reaction layer was prepared by a controlled drop-casting approach with 5 mg ZCN dispersed in 200 μL 0.5% Nafion solution uniformly.

The photocatalytic performance of the all-in-one structure was collected with a xenon lamp (PLS-SXE300D, Beijing Perfect Light Technology Co. Ltd) fitted with an AM1.5G filter to simulate the solar irradiance. Xenon lamp shines down from the top, and there is no shade between two layers. An all-in-one structure was put on the water. After reacting several hours under illumination, the concentration of product, H2O2 solution was detected through potassium permanganate titration [46].

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was performed on a Gemini 500 microscope (Carl Zeiss, Germany) at 8 kV. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were observed using the FEI Tecnai F20 electron microscope (FEI, America) under an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. Absorption spectra were obtained using a UV/VIS/NIR Lambda 950 spectrometer (PerkinElmer, America). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a Hyperion spectrometer (Bruker, Germany) at the scan range of 4000–400 cm−1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was collected by an Escalab 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, America) equipped with a monochromatic Al Kα radiation source. Real-time infrared thermal images were collected by using an infrared camera (Fotric 225, USA) to detect the temperature evolution on the evaporation structure and catalytic reaction layer interface. Simulated sunlight with a xenon lamp (PLS-SXE300D, 300 W, Beijing Perfect Light Technology Co. Ltd) fitted with an AM1.5G filter was used, and light intensity was measured by an optical power meter (PM400K5, Thorlabs, USA).

Catalyst ZCN was obtained by mixing C3N4 with ZIF-8 after heating in a tube furnace. The morphology of C3N4 and ZIF-8 was observed through scanning electron micrograph (SEM) (Fig. S1). C3N4 shows a characteristically sheet-like structure, and ZIF-8 exhibits a typically rhombic dodecahedra structure with the size of about 100 nm. And from SEM and TEM images (Fig. 2 a–e, c), ZIF-8 nanoparticles are loaded on the C3N4 visibly. Moreover, HAADF-STEM images of ZCN display the corresponding elemental mapping images, indicating the uniform distribution of C, N and Zn elements.

Besides, X-ray diffraction (XRD) was used to study the crystal structures of sample C3N4, ZIF-8 and ZCN in Fig. 2f. As for C3N4 (black curve), the peak at 12.7° corresponds to (100) crystal planes, which is related to the interplanar structural packing, and the sharp diffraction peak at 27.9° matches the (002) crystal plane, indicating the existence of interlayer stacking structure of aromatic systems. And for XRD spectrum of ZIF-8 (blue curve), there are four distinct diffraction peaks at 2θ = 7.3, 10.4, 12.7 and 18.0°, which can be ascribed to the (011), (002), (112) and (222) reflections, respectively. Both characteristic peaks of C3N4 and ZIF-8 are existed in the spectra of ZCN (red curve), indicating the recombination of C3N4 and ZIF-8. Furthermore, the chemical structure of ZCN was analyzed by XPS and FT-IR. From XPS survey spectrum of ZCN (Fig. 2g–i, S2), the peaks of C, N, O and Zn elements reveal the combination of C3N4 and ZIF-8. And the peaks (1044.3 eV for Zn 2p1/2, 1021.2 eV for Zn 2p3/2, 287.4 eV for N-C = N, 286.2 eV for C-N, 284.6 eV for CC, 400.2 eV for CNH, 399.0 eV for N-(C)3, and 397.8 eV for CNC) are consistent with the characteristic peaks of ZCN previously reported [47]. FT-IR was further applied to study the chemical bonding of samples C3N4, ZIF-8 and ZCN. From Fig. S3a, the peak of 3276 cm−1 and 3175 cm−1 are ascribed to NH2 and NH stretching vibration. And the peak of 1633 cm−1 and 1224 cm−1 belong to stretching vibration modes of CN heterocycles. And 813 cm−1 is ascribed to the breathing mode of triazine units (characteristic peaks for C3N4, black curve). And for the signal from ZIF-8, the peak located at 1143 cm−1 and 992 cm−1 are attributed to the C-N stretching vibration in imidazole rings, and typical Zn-N stretching mode is shown at 419 cm−1 (blue curve). All of the characteristic peaks of C3N4 and ZIF-8 appear in the ZCN (red curve), confirming the combination of C3N4 and ZIF-8. The results from FT-IR are in accordance with the analysis of XPS. And in Fig. S3b, UV–vis absorption spectrum shows that ZCN has a good visible light absorption ability, which indicates it has the potential as a good photocatalyst.

CDs were synthesized by the approach of electrochemically etching graphite rods, and the morphology of CDs was observed with transmission electron microscope (TEM). From TEM image (Fig. 3 b), evenly distributed spherical shape of CDs with the size 2 ~ 3 nm is shown, and the interplanar lattice spacing is 0.21 nm, assigned to the (100) diffraction of graphitic carbon. The CDs-based evaporator (CDE) was prepared by blade-casting CDs slurry on cellulose paper uniformly, and followed by thermal treatment with hydrazine hydrate vapor. For comparison of the difference of the chemical structure of CDs before and after treatment, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were applied. FT-IR spectra (Fig. S4) show peaks for hydroxyl O–H (3452 cm−1), carboxyl CO (1638 cm−1), aromatic CC (1390 cm−1) and epoxide COC (1116 cm−1) groups of CDs, indicating the successful electrochemical oxidation of graphite [43], while epoxy group peak in treated CDs is much weaker, which is consistent with XPS results (Fig. S5, Table S1). From XPS data, C/O ratio increases obviously after reduction process (2.38 for CDs, 4.01 for rCDs) and the content of C-O (18.14% for CDs, 14.02% for rCDs) decreases visibly, showing the partial removal of oxygen-containing groups and thus expansion of the p-conjugation domain in the rCDs.
Then all-in-one H2O2 generation device was prepared in bilayer structure, including CDs-based evaporation layer and photocatalysis layer with ZCN as catalyst.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Function of each part in an all-in-one device
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 3a, a 3D all-in-one device is fabricated by two cones. To clarify the mechanism of photocatalytic water splitting for H2O2 generation over ZCN, electron spin resonance (ESR) analysis was conducted. From Fig. S6, neither O2 − nor ⋅OH is detected in dark, while both two reactive oxygen species are shown under irradiation, indicating that H2O2 generates with ZCN through the dual-channel photocatalytic water splitting with the photo-induced electrons reducing O2 to H2O2 and the holes oxidizing H2O to H2O2, and the mechanism diagram is shown in Fig. S7. From Fig. 3d, ZCN dispersed in water shows excellent H2O2 generation performance of 2464 μmol g−1h−1 under the global air mass 1.5 (AM1.5G) simulated sunlight (1 sun) irradiation at normal pressure without any sacrificial agent. However, after being fixed on the cellulose paper, photocatalytic ability falls to 1728 μmol g−1h−1. And as shown in Fig. 3e, 3D water evaporation layer exhibits efficient solar steam generation capacity of 2.85 kg m−2h−1, which is 7.5 times the evaporation rate of pure water without any evaporators, due to the excellent absorption of full solar spectrum of CDE (Fig. S8).

Combining catalysis layer of awesome H2O2 generation ability with water evaporation layer of effective vapor generation property, the all-in-one photocatalytic device shows great production of H2O2 by achieving multi-functions integrating reaction, separation and concentration simultaneously. As shown in Fig. 4 a, Table S2, H2O2 solution concentration keeps increasing and it can reach 9.37 mmol L−1 after reacting for 5 h, and H2O2 evolution is 24.02 μmol. However, when the reaction time increased to 7 h, although H2O2 concentration increases, H2O2 evolution decreases obviously. Because H2O2 loss is quite large when there is little solution remaining. Temperature variations of different parts of all-in-one devices under reaction were recorded in situ to study the kinetic process of solar evaporation and photocatalysis. From Fig. 4b, within 600 s, the temperature of the tip of evaporator surface increases from 20.5 to 22.4 °C, and then remains almost invariable, showing that heat dissipation by water evaporation and heat acquisition from solar thermal conversion is in a relative balance state. For the bottom of CDE, temperature increases rapidly from 22.9 to 29.3 °C under illumination for 600 s, and then further increases to 31.6 °C, indicating the brilliant photothermal conversion abilities of CDE and great heat localization performance, realizing slight heat loss, and efficient water evaporation as well (Fig. 4c). And in Fig. S9, compared with temperature of all-in-one photocatalytic device, thermal image of photocatalytic structure without photothermic materials shows lower temperature at evaporator part after illumination for 40 min under 1 sun, which illustrates the rise in temperature is indeed due to photothermic materials.

To evaluate the H2O2 concentration performance and its influencing factors, several all-in-one devices with different evaporators of varied heights were fabricated. Among the CDE heights of 0, 1, 3, 5 cm (Fig. 5 a, Table S3 and S4), equipped with the same catalysis layer, the all-in-one device with 3 cm CDE shows the best H2O2 solution concentration performance of 5.50 mmol L−1, and the amount of H2O2 generation can reach to 18.71 μmol under simulated solar illumination of 1 sun for 3 h. However, under the same conditions, all-in-one devices with a height lower than 3 cm show inferior H2O2 concentration capability (4.38 mmol L−1 for no CDE, 4.93 mmol L−1 for CDE height of 1 cm) while quite large H2O2 evolution (18.65 μmol for system without CDE, 19.14 μmol for system with CDE height of 1 cm), indicating 3 cm CDE provides great evaporation ability as well as excellent concentration performance. In addition, CDE of 5 cm (H2O2 concentration of 4.96 mmol L−1, H2O2 evolution of 15.62 μmol) is so high and lead to inevitable H2O2 loss during concentration process, make the amount of H2O2 in the remaining solution less than other experimental groups with lower CDE, which is not conducive to realize H2O2 generation with high concentration. To further study the experimental results in different CDE heights, the real-time temperatures of these all-in-one devices are monitored under the simulated solar flux of 1 sun. After illuminating 40 min, the equilibrium temperatures of the CDE tip have evident difference (Fig. 5b, 31.2 °C for H = 1 cm, 24.0 °C for H = 3 cm, 22.3 °C for H = 5 cm) due to different thermal equilibrium states led by the degree of the angle at the top of the cone. The small angles of the top of the cone lead to low temperature even lower than ambient temperature, hence CDE can obtain environmental heat to improve water evaporation [48,49]. And the temperature of CDE bottom is almost the same for H = 3 and H = 5 cm, while the temperature of CDE bottom of H = 1 cm is a little higher at the steady-state situation (Fig. S10). Besides, the temperatures of catalyst layer for all four all-in-one devices are nearly the same, about 30 °C, indicating the height of CDE has little effect on the temperature of catalyst surface and thus, has no effect on the catalysis reaction.
To evaluate the relationship between water evaporation and H2O2 concentration, mathematical analysis was conducted as follows.

According to [50]
(1)
Wx = ( W - d W ) ( x - d x ) + y d W

Then, we get
(2)

= 1.105 8 ln

( 25 ° C )

Supposed the initial amount of solution to be W1, it will be W2 after concentrating. Then the initial H2O2 mole fraction is x1, it turns to be x2 after concentrating. The amount of solution is labeled as W during the reaction. And H2O2 mole fraction in solution is x, while H2O2 mole fraction in the vapor is y.

From the above theoretical analysis, in addition to the intrinsic catalysis ability of the photocatalyst, the increment of concentration is largely related to the amount of water evaporation. And the solar intensity plays an important role in evaporation, then experiments under different solar flux were conducted (Fig. 5c, Table S5). All-in-one device doesn’t work in dark (Fig. S11). And compared with the device without evaporation layer, the mass of water evaporated with all-in-one integrated catalytic device increases obviously, and H2O2 concentration increases evidently. Among H2O2 generation under illumination with different solar intensities, the reaction occurred under 2 sun shows the best H2O2 concentration performance. Mole fraction of H2O2 solution obtained through all-in-one device working is 1.86 times more concentrated than that of not concentrated H2O2 solution under 2 sun (1.14 times under 0.3 sun, 1.18 times under 0.5 sun, 1.26 times under 1 sun), which is corresponding to the results of theoretical analysis. Fig. 5d shows good stability of this all-in-one device, exhibiting that all-in-one device can be reused for at least ten cycles (each reaction time is 3 h) under the simulated solar flux of 1 sun.

The capacity of multiple devices affording H2O2 generation, separation and concentration in one step are assessed. As shown in Fig. 6 a and Table S6, catalysis and concentration performance of the different number of devices from one to five are studied. With increasing the number of devices, photocatalysis area and evaporation area multiply (Table S7), the amount of applied catalyst and initial water increase correspondingly, and the amount of H2O2 evolution increases proportionally while H2O2 solution concentrations are almost the same, which holding promise for large-scale application. And to further study the H2O2 generation and concentration performance for multiple devices, assembled quadruple devices are applied. As shown in Fig. 6b and Table S8, both H2O2 solution concentration and evolution increase in 5 h, which are in line with the H2O2 production and concentration ability of a single device. And the H2O2 solution concentration reaches 13.13 mmol L−1, the amount of H2O2 generated is 88.70 μmol after illumination for 5 h (5.49 mmol L−1, 62.21 μmol for 3 h). From Table S9, compared with other studies about H2O2 photoproduction, all-in-one device shows remarkable H2O2 concentration performance. And the real-time temperature change of the tip, the bottom of the CDE and catalyst surface is recorded to study the effect of temperature on catalysis and concentration properties (Fig. 6c, S12). After illumination 40 min, temperature of CDE tip increases from 22.1 °C to 25.7 °C, temperature of CDE bottom increases from 23.6 °C to 32.0 °C, and temperature of catalyst surface increases from 25.1 °C to 31.0 °C. The balance temperatures of the three parts of quadruple devices are almost the same as temperature of matching sites at a single device.

To further study the relations between catalysis and evaporation, the simplified 2D structure is applied. Different proportion of catalysis area and evaporation area is conducted. With the increment of catalysis area, H2O2 generation and solution concentration increasing gradually (Fig. 7 a, Table S10), due to catalysis part playing a central role in H2O2 generation. Based on ensuring the catalysis area as big as possible, the best way to get large evaporation is to build a 3D structure evaporator, then fabricate a 3D all-in-one photocatalytic device. In addition, mixtures of catalyst with different content of CDs is drop-casted on cellulose paper directly. With increasing of CDs content from 0% to 16.7% (mass fraction), H2O2 evolution and solution concentration decrease obviously, and when it increases to 37.5%, H2O2 evolution and solution concentration are almost the same as that of the device of 16.7% CDs content (Fig. 7b). As shown in Fig. S13, adding CDs to the content of 16.7%, the catalysis surface turns to black evidently, whereas increasing to CDs content of 37.5%, there is no perceptible contrast between devices of CDs content of 16.7% and 37.5%. And from Fig. 7c, d, temperature changes of sample surface are recorded in situ. Without CDs, the surface temperature changes from 23.2 °C to 35.1 °C, while the surface temperature of the sample containing 16.7% CDs changes from 23.4 °C to 41.2 °C, and the surface temperature of the sample containing 37.5% CDs changes from 23.0 °C to 41.4 °C, which is similar as that of the sample containing 16.7% CDs. It shows the increment of the temperature of catalysis layer is not conducive to this photocatalysis reaction, because the relatively high temperature accelerates the recombination of electrons and holes [37–39].

In addition, to reveal the H2O2 distribution and temperature distribution in this photocatalysis system, COMSOL Multiphysics software was applied to simulate different states under illumination and light absorption abilities of photocatalysis layer and water evaporation layer have been considered (Fig. S14). The model structure is shown in Fig. S15, with red cone denoting evaporator, orange loop denoting catalysis layer, as well as blue bulk denoting water bulk. And the H2O2 concentration distribution varies with time because of H2O2 photoproduction and solar water evaporation. In Fig. S16, H2O2 concentration in the remaining solution increases visibly and tip aggregation becomes more obvious with increment of irradiation time. Fig. 8 a shows H2O2 concentration distribution with radius of the evaporation cone of 0.5 cm under 1 sun illumination after 3 h, and H2O2 concentration of the remaining solution is about 5.65 mmol L−1, which is in line with experimental results, 5.50 mmol L−1. Besides, the tip of evaporator exhibits significant H2O2 aggregation due to excellent water evaporation performance. And Fig. 8b shows temperature distribution of this structure, in which the tip of evaporator shows lower temperature while the temperature of the bottom is higher, corresponding to thermal images of experimental results (Fig. 4c). To be more specific, isoconcentration diagram of the H2O2 concentration distribution and isothermal diagram of the temperature distribution under 1 sun illumination after 3 h is shown in Fig. S17. To further study the effect of photocatalysis area and evaporation area, various 3D all-in-one device models with different radius of the evaporation cone while the same all-in-one device projected area are built (Fig. S18) with other model conditions remaining the same. As shown in Figs. 8c and S19, with the increment of the radius of the evaporation cone (from 0.25 cm to 1.30 cm), that is, the increment of evaporation area, H2O2 concentration keeps increasing after illumination for the same time. Instead, H2O2 evolution keeps increasing noticeably for the radius of the evaporation cone of 0.25 cm and 0.50 cm within 3 h, and for the radius of the evaporation cone of 1.00 cm, the increase of H2O2 evolution has slowed down after illumination for 2 h, while H2O2 evolution even decreases for the radius of the evaporation cone of 1.30 cm after illumination for 2 h. Considering both results of H2O2 concentration and H2O2 evolution, the device with the radius of the evaporation cone between 0.50 cm and 1.00 cm performs better for high concentration H2O2 generation. From Fig. 8d, H2O2 concentration keeps increasing with all-in-one devices of increasing radius of the evaporation cone under 1 sun illumination after 3 h, while H2O2 evolution decreases experimentally, which is consistent with the change of simulative values by COMSOL.

To be more accurately describing the relations between H2O2 concentration, photocatalysis area and evaporation area, a theoretical mathematical expression is derived.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Merits and improvements about all-in-one device
ce:section.ce:para: As discussed above, the combination of solar-driven photocatalysis and solar water evaporation is the preferred work principle for the development of all-in-one photocatalytic devices. Such integrated devices have the opportunity to maximize the use of solar energy. For photocatalysis performance, the utilization of solar energy depends on the spectral absorption of the catalyst and the thermodynamic limitations of the catalysis reaction. The remaining long-wavelength sunlight, as well as the sunlight scattered by the photocatalyst, could be used for photothermal conversion. As for this all-in-one photocatalysis device, constructed through combination of two separate parts, photothermal cone for water evaporation as well as photocatalytic loop for H2O2 generation, realizing high concentration H2O2 direct photoproduction without separation and concentration process. As the result of integrated photocatalysis and water evaporation, H2O2 evolution reaches 17.13 μmol and H2O2 solution concentration gets to 10.15 mmol L−1 under simulated sunlight with solar flux of 2 sun irradiation for 3 h. And then mole fraction of H2O2 solution obtained through all-in-one device working is 1.86 times more concentrated than that of not concentrated H2O2 solution.

Although high concentration H2O2 direct photoproduction without separation and concentration process is achieved, it should be noted that this integrated photocatalytic device, whether in terms of structure or performance, still has a lot of room for improvement to meet industrial production requirements. To be specific, more optimized structural design to achieve better solar absorption capacity still needs to improve. And besides, photocatalyst with excellent performance and solar absorber materials with awesome photothermal property, or other composites of multifunctional materials also need further study to realize photoproduction of H2O2 in industry.

In this work, an all-in-one photocatalysis device was prepared with bilayer structure, catalysis layer used for H2O2 generation and water evaporation layer to realize H2O2 solution concentration. As the result of ingenious design, with a single one-in-one device, mole fraction of H2O2 solution obtained is 1.86 times more concentrated than that of the device without evaporator part after illumination for 3 h with simulated solar flux of 2 sun, corresponding to the theoretical analysis under the same situation, and H2O2 evolution reaches to 17.13 μmol and H2O2 solution concentration can get to 10.15 mmol L−1. Furthermore, COMSOL Multiphysics software was applied to simulate H2O2 distribution and temperature distribution in this photocatalysis system and analyze the relation between device structure and performance. Since multiple all-in-one photocatalysis devices can work simultaneously, making H2O2 photoproduction closer to practical application. Above all, the design of this photocatalytic device not only provides insight for a photocatalysis system integrating reaction, separation and concentration in one step in the field of photocatalysis systems requiring liquid product separation, but also expands the application of water evaporation.
